title: Separatism. In the past, some Christians fell into the separatist trap. They responded to culture with condemnation and retreat. Removing themselves far away from the corruption of culture is the name of their game.
scripture_ref: ‘’When Jesus had finished saying all this the people who were listening, he entered Capernaum. There a centurion’s servant, whom his master valued highly, was sick and about to die. The centurion heard of Jesus and sent some elders of the Jews to him, asking him to come and heal his servant. When they came to Jesus, they pleaded earnestly with him, “This man deserves to have you do this, because he loves our nation and has built our synagogue.” So Jesus went with them He was not far from the house when the centurion sent friends to say to him: “Lord, don’t trouble yourself, for I do not deserve to have you come under my roof. That is why I did not even consider myself worthy to come to you. But say the word, and my servant will be healed. For I myself am a man under authority, with soldiers under me. I tell this one, ‘Go,’ and he goes; and that one, ‘Come,’ and he comes. I say to my servant, ‘Do this,’ and he does it.” When Jesus heard this, he was amazed at him, and turning to the crowd following him, he said, “I tell you, I have not found such great faith even in Israel.” Then the men who had been sent, returned to the house and found the servant well.’’
scripture_text: There have been three broad ways Christians have reacted to society: -
devotional:
Antagonism. Some Christians see little in the current culture worth redeeming and have decided to fight against almost everything that culture promotes. They succeed in stating clearly what they are against, but their Achilles heel is suggesting alternatives that embody what they are for.

Relevance. Others have gone to the opposite extreme by falling into the “relevance trap.” In an effort to appeal to outsiders, some Christians simply copy culture. They become a photocopy of what they perceive as hip, in the hope that people will perceive them — and their organizations, ministries, and churches — as “cool.”

None of these three approaches are helpful, a better way is countercultural Christianity.

What is countercultural Christianity?

It is the advancement of the common good in society. It is being salt, a preserving agent actively working for restoration in the middle of a decaying culture. Countercultural Christians attach themselves to people and structures that are in danger of rotting while availing themselves to Christ’s redeeming power to do work through them.

We are going to look at the story of the Roman centurion and the healing of his servant. Luke places this story at the end of the Sermon on the Plain as a tangible, flesh and blood example of what it means to be countercultural, what it means to swim against the tide, what it means to demonstrate the distinctiveness of the Kingdom of God.

A. Countercultural living is an expression of authentic faith.

In this passage, Jesus implicitly redefines faith. We often have a one dimensional understanding of faith. We understand faith as a set of beliefs, a set of truths we hold onto, or a person we trust. Now all that is true but incomplete. Faith is how we choose to live because of what we believe. You could put this way, any faith in God that does not determine and define how we live is not authentic faith. Faith is the outworking of our beliefs and trust in God through a distinctive lifestyle that confronts and confounds the norms of a godless society.
prayer: Prayer, Father help me to live a life that is different to the world but not odd or offensive.

Separatism. In the past, some Christians fell into the separatist trap. They responded to culture with condemnation and retreat. Removing themselves far away from the corruption of culture is the name of their game.

‘’When Jesus had finished saying all this the people who were listening, he entered Capernaum. There a centurion’s servant, whom his master valued highly, was sick and about to die. The centurion heard of Jesus and sent some elders of the Jews to him, asking him to come and heal his servant. When they came to Jesus, they pleaded earnestly with him, “This man deserves to have you do this, because he loves our nation and has built our synagogue.” So Jesus went with them He was not far from the house when the centurion sent friends to say to him: “Lord, don’t trouble yourself, for I do not deserve to have you come under my roof. That is why I did not even consider myself worthy to come to you. But say the word, and my servant will be healed. For I myself am a man under authority, with soldiers under me. I tell this one, ‘Go,’ and he goes; and that one, ‘Come,’ and he comes. I say to my servant, ‘Do this,’ and he does it.” When Jesus heard this, he was amazed at him, and turning to the crowd following him, he said, “I tell you, I have not found such great faith even in Israel.” Then the men who had been sent, returned to the house and found the servant well.’’
There have been three broad ways Christians have reacted to society: -

Antagonism. Some Christians see little in the current culture worth redeeming and have decided to fight against almost everything that culture promotes. They succeed in stating clearly what they are against, but their Achilles heel is suggesting alternatives that embody what they are for.

Relevance. Others have gone to the opposite extreme by falling into the “relevance trap.” In an effort to appeal to outsiders, some Christians simply copy culture. They become a photocopy of what they perceive as hip, in the hope that people will perceive them — and their organizations, ministries, and churches — as “cool.”

None of these three approaches are helpful, a better way is countercultural Christianity.

What is countercultural Christianity?

It is the advancement of the common good in society. It is being salt, a preserving agent actively working for restoration in the middle of a decaying culture. Countercultural Christians attach themselves to people and structures that are in danger of rotting while availing themselves to Christ’s redeeming power to do work through them.

We are going to look at the story of the Roman centurion and the healing of his servant. Luke places this story at the end of the Sermon on the Plain as a tangible, flesh and blood example of what it means to be countercultural, what it means to swim against the tide, what it means to demonstrate the distinctiveness of the Kingdom of God.

A. Countercultural living is an expression of authentic faith.

In this passage, Jesus implicitly redefines faith. We often have a one dimensional understanding of faith. We understand faith as a set of beliefs, a set of truths we hold onto, or a person we trust. Now all that is true but incomplete. Faith is how we choose to live because of what we believe. You could put this way, any faith in God that does not determine and define how we live is not authentic faith. Faith is the outworking of our beliefs and trust in God through a distinctive lifestyle that confronts and confounds the norms of a godless society.

Prayer: Prayer, Father help me to live a life that is different to the world but not odd or offensive.
Bible in a year reading plan: - Joshua 24 Judges 1-2
